expectations.evidence: Found all required headings in SPEC.md: ## Problem (line 12), ## Solution (16), ## Scope (20), ## Design Decisions (40), ### Failure Modes (58), ## Risks & Open Questions (65), ## Alternatives Considered (73), ## Verification (80).

expectations.text: No code fences in the spec outside Implementation Notes
expectations.evidence: Search for triple backticks in SPEC.md returned no matches.

expectations.text: No TypeScript interface/type definitions in Design Decisions
expectations.evidence: Design Decisions (lines 42-56) contains prose paragraphs; no interface/type declarations or field-type syntax definitions.

expectations.text: No proto message definitions or field listings in Design Decisions
expectations.evidence: Design Decisions discusses contract shape in prose (line 42) and does not include proto message blocks or numbered field/type listings.

expectations.text: Scope items are capabilities, not file paths
expectations.evidence: In Scope bullets (lines 24-30) describe user-visible capabilities; no exact file paths or 'NEW: src/...' items appear there.

expectations.text: Spec is under 120 lines excluding Implementation Notes
expectations.evidence: SPEC.md has 114 total lines, which is below the 120-line limit.

expectations.text: Design Decisions use bold topic sentences
expectations.evidence: All Design Decisions paragraphs begin with bold opener sentences (lines 42, 44, 46, 48, 50, 52, 54, 56), exceeding the >80% threshold.

expectations.text: Implementer could start without asking questions
expectations.evidence: Core areas are covered: data model/API contract (line 42), rendering approach for time-series overlays (48), URL param selection (46), panel context menu integration (50), annotations tab behavior (52), and chat tool behavior (54-56).

expectations.text: Design Decisions contains decisions not implementation
expectations.evidence: Design Decisions includes implementation-level hook details and concrete technical mechanisms: `useDashboard` and `useDashboardViewStore` (line 44), `useRegisterChatTools` and `ToolResult` (line 54), plus explicit path detail `src/api/proto/*.ts` (line 42). Per grading rule, hook details/implementation leaks are a FAIL.

Failure mode bullets (lines 60-63) make explicit policy choices (clear selection with non-blocking notice, eventual consistency preference, preserve out-of-range selection, reject invalid bounds without auto-correct). >50% are genuine policy decisions.

expectations.text: Scope items describe behaviors not files
expectations.evidence: All listed In Scope items (7/7, lines 24-30) are behavioral capabilities; none are file/component implementation directives.

execution_metrics.evidence: No metrics.json present. outputs directory contains only SPEC.md.

claims.claim: Annotations are persisted per dashboard and fetched via dashboard-specific APIs.
claims.evidence: Stated directly in In Scope bullet: line 26.

claims.claim: Selecting an annotation updates URL params for shareable deep links.
claims.evidence: Stated in In Scope line 29 and expanded in Design Decisions line 46.

claims.claim: Chat tools support create, list, and navigate/select annotation actions.
claims.evidence: Specified in In Scope line 30 and Design Decisions line 56.

claims.claim: The narrowed chat tool scope keeps first delivery low-risk.
claims.evidence: Risk reduction is asserted (lines 36 and 56) but not empirically demonstrated within the spec artifact.

suggestions.assertion: No TypeScript interface/type definitions in Design Decisions
suggestions.reason: This partially overlaps with the broader no_implementation_leaks assertion; consider consolidating to reduce duplicate checks while keeping the stricter hard-fail semantics.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertion set is strong and discriminating for structure and anti-leak constraints; minor overlap between two implementation-leak-related assertions.
